title: Who builds the object you injected?

options: You do — by calling `new StudentService(new StudentRepository())` in the main method
The JVM creates it automatically the moment the class is loaded
The Spring container creates both beans and injects the repository into the constructor
The repository creates itself using a static initializer block
mcq.explanation_md: This is dependency injection in one picture: the Spring container builds both objects and hands the repository into the constructor — your code never calls `new` for either. The first option is tempting because that *is* how plain Java works, and it would even compile and run — but then Spring manages nothing, and every Spring feature (injection inside the repository, transactions, configuration) silently stops working. The JVM loading a class doesn't construct instances, and static blocks run code but don't wire dependencies.
